Commercial Mar 28, 2026

5 Airlines With The Largest Boeing 787 Fleets In 2026

The aircraft is becoming the backbone of many airlines' fleets.

The 787's dominance in widebody fleets signals airlines' commitment to fuel efficiency and long-haul capacity as they navigate post-pandemic recovery and route expansion.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Mar 28, 2026

Just 222 Seats: United Airlines' 4 Ultra-Premium Boeing 787 Routes In 2026

The new layout will initially be deployed domestically, with its international rollout coming next month.

United's strategic deployment of ultra-premium 787s on domestic routes signals a shift toward premium revenue concentration on high-value domestic markets before expanding internationally.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Mar 28, 2026

5 US Airports Undergoing Major Improvement Projects

These facilities are continuing to expand.

Strategic airport infrastructure investments signal continued capacity constraints and competitive positioning for post-pandemic demand growth.

See Simple Flying →

Military Mar 28, 2026

Northrop Grumman Testbed With AIM-260-Like Nose Spotted Flying Alongside Raytheon’s VOODOO 1 at Eglin AFB

The two testbeds, known by their callsigns SCAN 06 and VOODOO 1, carried out concurrent operations in Eglin’s highly instrumented airspace. For three consecutive days, on Mar. 24, 25 and 26, two testbed aircraft, the Northrop Grumman CRJ-700 carrying the U.S. registration code N806X and typically...

Concurrent testing of next-generation air-to-air missile systems by competing contractors suggests accelerated development timelines for advanced air combat capabilities.

See The Aviationist →

PAI
Safety Mar 28, 2026

The Evolution of Aviation SMS

From the Wright brothers to the 2024 FAA rule — how aviation safety evolved from reactive investigation to proactive risk management.

Modern SMS shifts airlines from investigating accidents after the fact to systematically identifying and mitigating hazards before they cause incidents, fundamentally changing safety culture.

See PAI Consulting — SMS Quick Takes →

Commercial Mar 28, 2026

Why Airlines Choose Certain Aircraft For Routes

An almost endless array of factors must be considered when deciding which plane should fly where...

Route profitability optimization requires airlines to balance fuel efficiency, passenger demand, runway capabilities, and crew logistics rather than defaulting to largest available aircraft.

See Simple Flying →

Aerospace Mar 28, 2026

Starfish Space finds a new partner for docking demonstration mission

Starfish Space has changed plans for a docking demonstration mission after its original partner backed out.

Starfish Space's ability to secure alternative partners for critical orbital servicing missions demonstrates the growing market viability of in-space logistics infrastructure despite setbacks.

See SpaceNews →

Military Mar 28, 2026

Germany Considering MQ-28A Ghost Bat CCA, Says Defense Minister

German Defense Minister Boris Pistorius said the MQ-28 Ghost Bat is among the uncrewed aircraft being evaluated to support the Eurofighter Typhoon fighter fleet. Germany’s defense minister Boris Pistorius said during his visit to Australia on Mar. 27, 2026, that Berlin is considering Boeing Defence...

Germany's potential adoption of the MQ-28A Ghost Bat would establish a NATO ally's operational reliance on Australian-designed autonomous combat systems, reshaping European air superiority partnerships.

See The Aviationist →

PAI
Regulatory Mar 28, 2026

Selecting Risk Controls and Accepting Residual Risk

Where Safety Risk Management moves from analysis to action — how organizations develop controls under §5.55(c)–(d).

Effective residual risk acceptance requires documented organizational protocols that balance control implementation costs against safety benefits rather than defaulting to maximum mitigation.

See PAI Consulting — SMS Quick Takes →

Commercial Mar 28, 2026

Dance For First Class? Delta Air Lines Gate Agent Holds Competition In Salt Lake City

Two passengers at Salt Lake City International Airport found themselves in a dance-off to win first class upgrades on a Delta Air Lines flight.

Delta's gamified upgrade strategy transforms routine gate operations into viral marketing moments, leveraging passenger engagement to enhance brand loyalty at zero incremental cost.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Mar 28, 2026

The 9 Longest Airbus A350 Routes Today

The aircraft offers exceptional long-range capabilities.

The A350's fuel efficiency and range enable airlines to profitably operate ultra-long-haul routes that were previously economically marginal, reshaping network planning strategies.

See Simple Flying →

Regulatory Mar 28, 2026

New Global Aviation Rules On Power Banks Come Into Effect – Here's What's Changing

Passengers can now carry up to two power banks each onboard.

The new two power-bank allowance per passenger represents a significant shift in IATA's lithium battery policy, reflecting growing device dependency while aviation safety standards remain intact.

See Simple Flying →

▶
Safety Mar 28, 2026

Flying in the Unforgiving Wire Environment (The Rotorcraft Collective)

Many pilots mistakenly believe they will have sufficient reaction time if they encounter power lines and other wires. Yet wire strikes have been among the top three causes of fatal helicopter...

Wire strike fatalities persist despite pilot overconfidence in reaction time, making proactive avoidance training and operational planning critical for rotorcraft safety.

See Federal Aviation Administration (YouTube) →

Commercial Mar 28, 2026

Why Do Some Airlines Still Operate 4-Engine Jets?

Airlines mostly operate legacy quadjets because they are affordable or they have no other choice. Emirates is unusual for just loving its A380s.

Four-engine aircraft economics hinge on fuel-efficient replacements being unavailable or unaffordable, making legacy quadjets a pragmatic fleet retention strategy for many carriers.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Mar 28, 2026

Delta Air Lines Holds Flight For Nearly An Hour For Late Passenger Related To Executive

Passengers on a Delta flight claim the aircraft was delayed to accommodate a late-arriving traveler allegedly connected to an airline executive.

Flight delays for VIP passengers erode crew scheduling integrity and competitive fairness, exposing airlines to operational inefficiency and passenger goodwill costs.

See Simple Flying →

Military Mar 28, 2026

USMC F-35Cs and USS Tripoli ARG Arrive in the Middle East

A month after the start of the hostilities, the U.S. Marine Corps is increasing its presence in the Middle East with the arrival of a F-35C squadron and the USS Tripoli Amphibious Ready Group. Mar. 28, 2026, marks a month since the U.S. and Israel launched Operations Epic Fury and Roaring Lion,...

The rapid deployment of F-35C carrier variants demonstrates the strategic value of naval aviation in power projection during regional conflicts.

See The Aviationist →

▶
General Aviation Mar 28, 2026

KSQL Noise Abatement Departure

The San Francisco Bay area is complicated airspace, and right in the middle of it is San Carlos Airport. San Carlos airport is surrounded by terrain, San Francisco International class bravo,...

San Carlos Airport's noise abatement procedures must balance residential proximity with complex Bay Area airspace constraints, requiring precise pilot technique during departures.

See Federal Aviation Administration (YouTube) →

Commercial Mar 28, 2026

Inside The Boeing 777X's Folding Wing Mechanism

Here's how Boeing's engineers have ensured the 777X's iconic folding wingtips can operate safely and reliably.

The 777X's folding wingtips enable gate compatibility with existing 777 infrastructure while maintaining aerodynamic efficiency of a larger aircraft, reducing airline operational complexity.

See Simple Flying →

Drones Mar 28, 2026

Robinson Unmanned

Robinson Unmanned (formerly Ascent AeroSystems) is the uncrewed aircraft systems (UAS) business unit of Robinson...

Robinson's pivot to dedicated UAS operations signals major helicopter manufacturers are actively competing in the commercial drone market rather than treating it as peripheral.

See sUAS News →

Drones Mar 28, 2026

Quantum Systems Strengthens Ukraine’s Air Defence Capabilities with 15,000 Interceptor Contract and WIY Drones Investment

In alignment with the defence priorities set out by the Ukrainian Government, Quantum Systems has...

Commercial drone manufacturers are pivoting toward defense contracts, signaling a structural shift in how unmanned systems funding and development priorities are being shaped by geopolitical conflict.

See sUAS News →

Industry Mar 28, 2026

Towards Environmentally Sustainable Aviation: A Review on Operational Optimization

Growing air traffic is straining airports and airspace while raising environmental concerns, and demand is only expected to increase. This paper reviews how advanced technologies and methods can expand capacity and improve efficiency across airline, airport, and flight operations. It also highlights emerging trends and gaps for future research in sustainable aviation.

Operational optimization through advanced technologies can simultaneously increase airport capacity and reduce environmental impact, addressing the dual pressure of rising demand and sustainability requirements.

See MDPI →

Regulatory Mar 28, 2026

Singapore delays SAF levy rollout amid Middle East conflict

As the war in Iran continues to roil world oil markets, Singapore has delayed the rollout of its…...

Singapore's SAF levy delay reflects how geopolitical disruptions to oil markets directly impact airlines' ability to absorb new surcharges during volatile fuel cost periods.

See AeroTime Hub →

Aerospace Mar 28, 2026

Artemis 2 astronauts arrive at KSC

The four Artemis 2 astronauts arrived in Florida March 27 for final preparations ahead of a launch still scheduled as soon as April 1.

Artemis 2's arrival at KSC marks the final operational phase for NASA's crewed lunar return mission, with launch readiness now dependent on last-minute vehicle and weather verification.

See SpaceNews →

Military Mar 27, 2026

[Updated] Iranian Attack on Prince Sultan Air Base Reportedly Struck U.S. KC-135s, E-3 Sentry

Satellite images show signs of an attack at Prince Sultan Air Base, Saudi Arabia, with The Wall Street Journal now reporting that an Iranian missile damaged “several U.S. refueling aircraft.” Satellite images emerged on Mar. 27, 2026, appeared to show signs of damage on an apron used by U.S....

Damage to KC-135 tankers directly impacts U.S. air operations' refueling capacity and mission availability across the region.

See The Aviationist →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

I Am Artemis: Erik Richards

Listen to this audio excerpt from Erik Richards, Near Space Network Mission Manager: For Erik Richards, supporting NASA’s first crewed Artemis mission to the Moon and back is the culmination of a career spent helping spacecraft communicate with Earth. Like many kids who grew up at the height of the...

Deep Space Network expertise accumulated over decades is now critical infrastructure enabling NASA's return to lunar exploration after a multi-decade gap.

See NASA →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

NASA Selects Intuitive Machines to Deliver Artemis Science, Tech to Moon

NASA has awarded Intuitive Machines of Houston, $180.4 million to deliver NASA-funded science and technology to the lunar surface as part of the agency’s CLPS (Commercial Lunar Payload Services) initiative and Artemis program. This lunar delivery, which includes seven payloads — five of them NASA’s...

NASA's $180.4M contract with Intuitive Machines signals growing reliance on commercial partners for lunar logistics, reducing development costs while accelerating Artemis timeline.

See NASA →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

NASA’s Environment and Energy “Blue Marble” Awards Categories

Category I: NASA Environmental Quality Award Recognizes excellence in environmental management and planning, including stewardship of natural and cultural resources. This category highlights achievements in compliance, conservation, remediation, communication, and environmental information...

NASA's environmental awards framework directly incentivizes aerospace contractors and facilities to integrate climate stewardship into operations, potentially reshaping sustainability standards across the aviation supply chain.

See NASA →

Safety Mar 27, 2026

EASA warns non-airworthy stolen engine parts could re-enter the market

The European Union Aviation Safety Agency (EASA) is warning airlines, maintenance providers and parts buyers to be on…...

Stolen aircraft engine components risk circumventing supply chain verification, potentially creating undocumented maintenance liabilities and airworthiness certification gaps across European operators.

See AeroTime Hub →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

Commercial Space Federation (CSF) Welcomes Two New Associate Members

March 27, 2026 – Washington, D.C.—The Commercial Space Federation (CSF) is pleased to welcome Astrolab and Zeno Power, two innovative companies advancing planetary mobility and reliable energy in extreme environments. […]...

The CSF's expansion to include lunar mobility and nuclear power solutions signals industry consolidation around deep-space infrastructure capabilities essential for sustained commercial operations beyond Earth orbit.

See SpaceNews →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

ESA to decide by June on Europe’s Gateway contributions

MUNICH — For more than a year, questions have swirled about Artemis changes and the Gateway’s role in U.S. lunar ambitions. Following NASA’s March 24 decision to halt work on Gateway, the lunar-orbiting station that had been intended to support astronauts before and after lunar surface missions,...

NASA's Gateway halt creates immediate pressure on ESA to clarify financial and technical commitments to lunar infrastructure before mid-2024, potentially reshaping European participation in Artemis.

See SpaceNews →

Military Mar 27, 2026

Gallery: Luke Days 2026

A glimpse back at last weekend's air show at Luke Air Force Base in Arizona.

Luke Air Force Base air shows demonstrate public engagement strategies that help military aviation maintain community support and recruit awareness among younger audiences.

See AVweb →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

NISAR’s View of Mount Rainier

Description This image captured by U.S.-Indian Earth satellite NISAR on Nov. 10, 2025, shows Washington’s Mount Rainier. The image is cropped from a much larger swath spanning the Pacific Northwest on a cloudy day; NISAR’s L-band SAR instrument is able to peer through the clouds at the surface...

NISAR's L-band SAR capability to penetrate cloud cover enables persistent Earth observation for aviation weather monitoring and terrain mapping regardless of atmospheric conditions.

See NASA →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

NISAR Views Mount St. Helens

Description This image captured by U.S.-Indian Earth satellite NISAR on Nov. 10, 2025, shows Washington’s Mount St. Helens. The image is cropped from a much larger swath spanning the Pacific Northwest on a cloudy day; NISAR’s L-band SAR instrument is able to peer through the clouds at the surface...

NISAR's L-band synthetic aperture radar demonstrates critical all-weather monitoring capabilities for volcanic and geological hazard assessment, enabling cloud-penetrating Earth observation that traditional optical satellites cannot achieve.

See NASA →

Safety Mar 27, 2026

NTSB Issues Preliminary Report on Hudson River Ditching

Investigators say an instructional flight ended after a loss of oil pressure and total engine failure.

Oil pressure loss on a single-engine aircraft over water highlights the critical need for improved engine monitoring systems and emergency procedures in training operations.

See AVweb →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

NASA Tech and Science Bound for Low Earth Orbit on Commercial Launch

Technology and science demonstrations, supported by various NASA industry collaborations and agency developments, are set to launch to low Earth orbit aboard a SpaceX Falcon 9 rocket as part of the company’s Transporter-16 commercial rideshare mission. These demonstrations will test thermal...

NASA's increasing reliance on commercial rideshare missions reduces launch costs while accelerating technology validation timelines for space-based experiments.

See NASA →

Regulatory Mar 27, 2026

House Transportation Chair Sam Graves To Retire

Graves helped shape FAA reauthorization, BasicMed and several other general aviation measures.

Graves' retirement removes a key legislative ally for general aviation priorities, potentially reshaping future FAA policy discussions and regulatory advocacy strategies.

See AVweb →
